title: Enums
subtitle: enum.Enum — familiar, with a few twists

sections.java: // Java enum: constants + optional fields public enum Status { ACTIVE, SUSPENDED, CLOSED } Status s = Status.ACTIVE; s.name(); // "ACTIVE" Status.valueOf("CLOSED");
sections.python: # Subclass Enum; values are explicit from enum import Enum, auto class Status(Enum): ACTIVE = auto() SUSPENDED = auto() CLOSED = auto() s = Status.ACTIVE s.name # "ACTIVE" (attribute, no parens) Status["CLOSED"] # by name, like valueOf

sections.title: Members Have a Name AND a Value
sections.content: Each member wraps a .name (the identifier) and a .value (what you assigned — auto() gives 1, 2, 3…). Look up by name with Status["CLOSED"] and by value with Status(3) — note the different brackets. Members are singletons, so compare with is (if s is Status.ACTIVE) or ==; never compare against the raw value unless you pulled .value.

sections.java: // Enum with data + behavior public enum Planet { EARTH(5.97e24), MARS(6.42e23); final double mass; Planet(double m) { mass = m; } double mass() { return mass; } }
sections.python: # Value can be a tuple; add methods freely class Planet(Enum): EARTH = 5.97e24 MARS = 6.42e23 def gravity(self) -> float: return 6.67e-11 * self.value Planet.EARTH.gravity()

sections.title: IntEnum and StrEnum for Interop
sections.content: Plain Enum members are not equal to their raw values (Status.ACTIVE != 1). When you need an enum that doubles as an int or str for JSON/DB/serialization, subclass IntEnum or StrEnum (3.11+): then Color.RED == "RED" is True and it serializes transparently. Use @enum.unique to forbid duplicate values, and iterate members simply with for s in Status:.

sections.question: How do you compare an enum member to check which one it is, idiomatically?
options: Compare to the integer value: s == 1
Use identity or equality against the member: s is Status.ACTIVE
sections.explanation: Enum members are singletons, so the idiomatic check is s is Status.ACTIVE (or ==). Comparing to the raw value (1 or "ACTIVE") fails for a plain Enum — that only works if you deliberately subclass IntEnum/StrEnum. There's no .equals() in Python.
